q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>}</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∈</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>A</mi></math> : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>|</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>|</mo></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="}" open=""><mrow><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be a relation on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. Then the minimum number of elements, that must be added to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> so that it becomes reflexive and symmetric, is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>}</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∈</mo><mi>A</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>A</mi></math> : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>|</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>|</mo></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="}" open=""><mrow><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be a relation on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. Then the minimum number of elements, that must be added to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> so that it becomes reflexive and symmetric, is
solution:
Given,

A={-4,-3,-2,0,1,3,4} and R={(a,b)∈A×A : b=|a| or b2=a+1 be a relation on A,

So, the relation is given by,

R={(-4,4),(-3,3),(0,0),(1,1),(3,3),(4,4),(0,1),(3,-2)}

Now, relation to be reflexive (a,a)∈R ∀ a∈A

⇒(-4,-4),(-3,-3),(-2,-2) also should be added in R.

Now relation to be symmetric if (a,b)∈R, then (b,a)∈R ∀ a,b∈A

⇒(4,-4),(3,-3),(1,0),(-2,3) also should be added in R

Hence, minimum number of elements to be added to
R=3+4=7

subject: Mathematics
q: The minimum number of elements that must be added to the relation R=(a,b),(b,c) on the set {a,b,c} so that it becomes symmetric and transitive is:
question: The minimum number of elements that must be added to the relation R=(a,b),(b,c) on the set {a,b,c} so that it becomes symmetric and transitive is:
solution:
Given:

A=a,b,c,d

And,

R=a,b,b,c

For symmetric relation, we must add b,a, c,b.

So, R=a,b,b,a, b,c,c,b.

For transitive relation, we must add

a,c, b,b, c,a, a,a, c,c

Hence, 

R=b,a, c,b, a,c, b,b, c,a, a,a, c,c, a,b, b,c

So, number of new elements added=7

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $X$ and $Y$ are two events such that $P(X \cup Y=) P X \cap(Y . \quad)$
Statement 1: $P\left(X \cap Y^{\prime}=\dot{P} X^{\prime} \cap(Y=0 \quad)\right.$
Statement 2: $P(X) P Y \in 2) P X \cap Y(\quad)$
question:
Let $X$ and $Y$ are two events such that $P(X \cup Y=) P X \cap(Y . \quad)$
Statement 1: $P\left(X \cap Y^{\prime}=\dot{P} X^{\prime} \cap(Y=0 \quad)\right.$
Statement 2: $P(X) P Y \in 2) P X \cap Y(\quad)$
options: Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true; Statement 2 is a correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution:
Let $X$ and $Y$ be two events such that
$$
P(X \cup Y)=P(X \cap Y)
$$
We know
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P(X \cup Y)=P(X)+P(Y)-P(X \cap Y) \\
& P(X \cap Y)=P(X)+P(Y)-P(X \cap Y) \\
& \text { (from }(1) \\
& \Rightarrow P(X)+P(Y)=2 P(X \cap Y)
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, Statement $-2$ is true.
Now, $P\left(X \cap Y^{\prime}\right)=P(X)-P(X \cap Y)$
and $P\left(X^{\prime} \cap Y\right)=P(Y)-P(X \cap Y)$
This implies statement-1 is also true.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let R be a relation defined on the set $\{1,2,3,4\} \times\{1,2,3,4\}$ by $\mathrm{R}=\{((a, b),(c, d)): 2 a+3 b=3 c+4 d\}$. Then the number of elements in R is
question: Let R be a relation defined on the set $\{1,2,3,4\} \times\{1,2,3,4\}$ by $\mathrm{R}=\{((a, b),(c, d)): 2 a+3 b=3 c+4 d\}$. Then the number of elements in R is
solution:
We need $2a+3b = 3c+4d$ where $a,b,c,d \in \{1,2,3,4\}$.
Possible values of $2a+3b$: range from $5$ to $20$.
Possible values of $3c+4d$: range from $7$ to $28$.
For each common value $k$, the number of elements in $R$ equals (number of $(a,b)$ with $2a+3b=k$) $\times$ (number of $(c,d)$ with $3c+4d=k$).
$k=7$: $(a,b)=(2,1)$, $(c,d)=(1,1)$ $\Rightarrow 1 \times 1=1$
$k=10$: $(2,2)$, $(2,1)$ $\Rightarrow 1$
$k=11$: $(1,3),(4,1)$, $(1,2)$ $\Rightarrow 2$
$k=13$: $(2,3)$, $(3,1)$ $\Rightarrow 1$
$k=14$: $(1,4),(4,2)$, $(2,2)$ $\Rightarrow 2$
$k=15$: $(3,3)$, $(1,3)$ $\Rightarrow 1$
$k=16$: $(2,4)$, $(4,1)$ $\Rightarrow 1$
$k=17$: $(4,3)$, $(3,2)$ $\Rightarrow 1$
$k=18$: $(3,4)$, $(2,3)$ $\Rightarrow 1$
$k=20$: $(4,4)$, $(4,2)$ $\Rightarrow 1$
Total $= 1+1+2+1+2+1+1+1+1+1 = 12$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of relations, defined on the set $\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}\}$, which are both reflexive and symmetric, is equal to:
question: The number of relations, defined on the set $\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}\}$, which are both reflexive and symmetric, is equal to:
solution:
For a set with $n = 4$ elements, reflexive condition requires all diagonal pairs $(a,a), (b,b), (c,c), (d,d)$ to be included.
For symmetric relations, non-diagonal pairs come in symmetric pairs. Number of such pairs = $\binom{4}{2} = 6$.
For each pair, we either include both elements or exclude both → 2 choices each.
Total reflexive and symmetric relations = $2^6 = 64$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4\}$. Let R be a relation on A defined by $x \mathrm{Ry}$ if and only if $2 x+y \leqslant 2$. Let $l$ be the number of elements in R. Let m and n be the minimum number of elements required to be added in $R$ to make it reflexive and symmetric relations respectively. Then $l+m+n$ is equal to :
question: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{-2,-1,0,1,2,3,4\}$. Let R be a relation on A defined by $x \mathrm{Ry}$ if and only if $2 x+y \leqslant 2$. Let $l$ be the number of elements in R. Let m and n be the minimum number of elements required to be added in $R$ to make it reflexive and symmetric relations respectively. Then $l+m+n$ is equal to :
solution:
Given $A = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $xRy$ iff $2x + y \leqslant 2$.
For $x = -2$: $y \leqslant 6$, all 7 elements valid (7 pairs).
For $x = -1$: $y \leqslant 4$, all 7 elements valid (7 pairs).
For $x = 0$: $y \leqslant 2 \Rightarrow y \in \{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ (5 pairs).
For $x = 1$: $y \leqslant 0 \Rightarrow y \in \{-2,-1,0\}$ (3 pairs).
For $x = 2$: $y \leqslant -2 \Rightarrow y \in \{-2\}$ (1 pair).
For $x = 3, 4$: no valid $y$.
So $l = 7+7+5+3+1 = 23$.
For reflexive: $(x,x) \in R \Rightarrow 3x \leqslant 2 \Rightarrow x \leqslant 2/3$. Already present for $x = -2, -1, 0$. Missing for $x = 1, 2, 3, 4$, so $m = 4$.
For symmetric: need $(y,x) \in R$ for every $(x,y) \in R$. Missing pairs: $(3,-2), (4,-2), (2,-1), (3,-1), (4,-1), (2,0)$, so $n = 6$.
$l + m + n = 23 + 4 + 6 = 33$.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Among the relations
S=a,b:a,b∈R-0,2+ab>0 and T=a,b:a,b∈R,a2-b2∈Z,
question:
Among the relations
S=a,b:a,b∈R-0,2+ab>0 and T=a,b:a,b∈R,a2-b2∈Z,
solution:
Given,

a, b ∈ I and relation T is defined as a2-b2∈I

Now if a2-b2∈I so b2-a2 will also be integer, for example if 52-42=9 is integer then 42-52=-9 is also a integer,

Now checking transitive,

If 42-32=7 is an integer, 32-22=5 is an integer then 42-22=12 is also an integer, hence we can say that T is symmetric and transitive,

 Now checking relation S which is defined as 2+ab>0,

So, if we replace ab by -19 then 2+ab>0 is true but we take ba=-9 for symmetric we get 2+ba=2-9=-7≯0 hence, the relation is not symmetric, 

Now checking transitive, now if 2+ab>0⇒ab>-2 and bc>-2 then we cannot say that ac>-2,

For example if we take 41>-2, 1-1>-2 then 4-1≯-2, hence it is not transitive,

Hence, we can say that  T is symmetric and S is not.

Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is:
question: Let a set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>∪</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>∪</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>∪</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>i</mi></msub><mo>∩</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>j</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>ϕ</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>≠</mo><mi>j</mi><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>j</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Define the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math>{<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>∈</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> if and only if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>i</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>i</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>k</mi></math>}. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is:
options: reflexive but not symmetric and transitive
solution:
Let A=1,2,3

Then R=1,1,1,2,1,32,1,2,2,2,33,1,3,23,3

which is an equivalence relation.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let W denote the words in the English dictionary. Define the relation R by :
$R=\{(x, y) \in W \times W \mid$ the words $x$ and $y$ have at least one letter in common $\}$. Then $R$ is
question:
Let W denote the words in the English dictionary. Define the relation R by :
$R=\{(x, y) \in W \times W \mid$ the words $x$ and $y$ have at least one letter in common $\}$. Then $R$ is
solution:
Clearly $(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{x}) \in \mathrm{R} \forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{W}$. So, $\mathrm{R}$ is reflexive.
Let $(x, y) \in R$, then $(y, x) \in R$ as $x$ and $y$ have at least one letter in common. So, $R$ is symmetric.
But $R$ is not transitive for example
Let $x=$ DELHI, $y=$ DWARKA and $z=$ PARK
then $(x, y) \in R$ and $(y, z) \in R$ but $(x, z) \notin R$.

solution:
Equivalence class of 1,-1 is a circle with centre at 0,0and radius =2

S=x,y|x2+y2=2

subject: Mathematics
q:
In a certain town, 25% of the families own a phone and 15% own a car; 65% families own neither a phone nor a car and 2000 families own both a car and a phone. Consider the following three statements:

i 5% families own both a car and a phone.

ii 35% families own either a car or a phone.

iii 40000 families live in the town.

Then,
question:
In a certain town, 25% of the families own a phone and 15% own a car; 65% families own neither a phone nor a car and 2000 families own both a car and a phone. Consider the following three statements:

i 5% families own both a car and a phone.

ii 35% families own either a car or a phone.

iii 40000 families live in the town.

Then,
solution:
Let set A contains families which own a phone and set B contain families which own a car.

If 65% families own neither a phone nor a car, then 35% will own either a phone or a car

∴nA∪B=35%

Also we know that

nA∪B=nA+nB-n(A∩B)

35=25+15-n(A∩B)

nA∩B=5%

5% families own both phone and car and it is given to be 2000 .
∴5%  of x=2000

5x100 =2000

x=40000

Hence, i, ii and iii are correct.

subject: Mathematics
q: A survey shows that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>73</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the persons working in an office like coffee, whereas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo>%</mo></math> like tea. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> denotes the percentage of them, who like both coffee and tea, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> cannot be:
question: A survey shows that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>73</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the persons working in an office like coffee, whereas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo>%</mo></math> like tea. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> denotes the percentage of them, who like both coffee and tea, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> cannot be:
solution:
nC=73, nT=65, nC∩T=x, 

Considering that value of nC∪T cannot exceed 100 in terms of percentage, we get

nC∪T≤100

⇒  nC+nT-nC∩T≤100

⇒  x≥38

Therefore, x≠36.

nC∩T≤minnC,nT ⇒ x≤65

⇒ 38≤x≤65

Hence x may assume values 63,54,38.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $S$ be the set of the first 11 natural numbers. Then the number of elements in $A=\{B \subseteq S: n(B) \geqslant 2$ and the product of all elements of $B$ is even $\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
question: Let $S$ be the set of the first 11 natural numbers. Then the number of elements in $A=\{B \subseteq S: n(B) \geqslant 2$ and the product of all elements of $B$ is even $\}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
solution:
The set $S = \{1, 2, 3, ..., 11\}$ has 11 elements.
We need to count subsets $B$ with $n(B) \geq 2$ and even product.
A product is even iff at least one element is even.
Equivalently, count complements: a product is odd iff all elements are odd.
The odd numbers in $S$ are $\{1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11\}$ (6 elements).
Subsets of odd numbers: $2^6 = 64$.
Among these, those with $n(B) \geq 2$ are all except empty set (1) and singletons (6): $64 - 7 = 57$.
Total subsets with $n(B) \geq 2$: $2^{11} - 1 - 11 = 2036$.
Subsets with even product and $n(B) \geq 2$: $2036 - 57 = 1979$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>≤</mo><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>≠</mo><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> Then on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math>:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>≤</mo><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>≠</mo><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> Then on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math>:
solution:
To check if R1 is transitive -

Let a=16, b=4, c=1

Here a-b=16-4≤13 holds true

now b-c=4−1≤13 ⇒3⩽13   also holds true

but a-c=16−1≰13

Hence R1 is not transitive

Similarly, to check transitivity of R2

Let a=16, b=4, c=3

Here a-b=16-4≠13 and b-c=4-3≠13

but a-c=16-3=13

Hence, R2 is also not transitive.

Hence neither R1 nor R2 is an equivalence relation

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $S=\mathbf{N} \cup\{0\}$. Define a relation $R$ from $S$ to $\mathbf{R}$ by :
$\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y): \log _{\mathrm{e}} y=x \log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right), x \in \mathrm{~S}, y \in \mathbf{R}\right\}$

Then, the sum of all the elements in the range of $R$ is equal to :
question:
Let $S=\mathbf{N} \cup\{0\}$. Define a relation $R$ from $S$ to $\mathbf{R}$ by :
$\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y): \log _{\mathrm{e}} y=x \log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{2}{5}\right), x \in \mathrm{~S}, y \in \mathbf{R}\right\}$

Then, the sum of all the elements in the range of $R$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & S=\{0,1,2,3 \ldots \ldots\} \\ & \log _y y=\log _e\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow y=\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^x\end{aligned}$
Required $\text { Sum }=1+\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^1+\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^2+\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^3+\ldots . .-=\frac{1}{1-\frac{2}{5}}=\frac{5}{3}$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be relations on the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo></math>{<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>p</mi><mi>n</mi></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>p</mi></math> is a prime and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is an integer} and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo></math>{<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>p</mi><mi>n</mi></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>p</mi></math> is a prime and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math>}. Then, the number of elements in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ____.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be relations on the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo></math>{<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>p</mi><mi>n</mi></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>p</mi></math> is a prime and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is an integer} and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo></math>{<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>p</mi><mi>n</mi></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>p</mi></math> is a prime and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math>}. Then, the number of elements in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ____.
solution:
Here, p,pn∈1,2,…50

Now p can take values

2,3,5,7,11,13,17,23,29,31,37,41,43 and 47.

∴ R1={2,20,2,21…2,25,3,30,…3,33,5,50,…5,52,7,70,…7,72,11,110,11,111,...47,470,47,471}

R2=2,20,2,21,3,30,3,31....47,470,47,471

Now R1-R2≡R1∩R2'

So R1-R2=2,22,2,23,2,24,2,25,3,32,3,33,5,52,7,72

∴ nR1-R2=8

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the relation R on the set $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ defined by $(a, b) \in R$ if and only if $1+ab > 0$. Then, among the statements:
I. The number of elements in R is 17
II. R is an equivalence relation
question:
Consider the relation R on the set $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ defined by $(a, b) \in R$ if and only if $1+ab > 0$. Then, among the statements:
I. The number of elements in R is 17
II. R is an equivalence relation
options: Both I and II are true
Neither I nor II is true
solution:
The set is $A = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$.

For Statement I, the relation is defined as $(a, b) \in R \iff 1 + ab > 0$.
The pairs $(a, b)$ that do not belong to $R$ satisfy $1 + ab \le 0 \Rightarrow ab \le -1$.
The pairs satisfying $ab \le -1$ are $(-2, 1), (-2, 2), (-1, 1), (-1, 2), (1, -2), (1, -1), (2, -2), (2, -1)$.
There are $8$ such pairs.
The total number of pairs in $A \times A$ is $5 \times 5 = 25$.
The number of elements in $R$ is $25 - 8 = 17$.
Thus, Statement I is true.

For Statement II, checking transitivity gives $(-2, 0) \in R$ since $1 + (-2)(0) = 1 > 0$.
Also, $(0, 2) \in R$ since $1 + (0)(2) = 1 > 0$.
However, $(-2, 2) \notin R$ since $1 + (-2)(2) = -3 \le 0$.
Since $R$ is not transitive, it is not an equivalence relation.
Thus, Statement II is false.

Answer: Only I is true

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of all the elements of the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>H</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>F</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>24</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: The sum of all the elements of the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mn>100</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mi>H</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>F</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>24</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Given S=1,2,3…100

Now finding the sum of S=100×1012

 Prime factors of 24=23×3

Let nA= Multiples of 2

nB= Multiples of 3

nA∩B= Multiples of 2 & 3

So nA∪B=nA+nB−nA∩B

To have H.C.F to be 1 we need to subtract the sum of multiples of 2 & 3 from sum of set S to get required answer,

So required answer

=100×1012− Sum of nA∪B

=100×1012-2×50×512+332102−162×102 =1633

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{" separators="|"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>50</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>X</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi>s </mi><mi>a </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi>e </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>X</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi>s </mi><mi>a </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi>e </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi>f </mi><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the number of elements in the smallest subset of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>, containing both <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, is.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{" separators="|"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>50</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>X</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi>s </mi><mi>a </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi>e </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>X</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>n </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi>s </mi><mi>a </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi>e </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi>f </mi><mn>7</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the number of elements in the smallest subset of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>, containing both <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>, is.
solution:
Here, A=2,4,6,....50 and B=7,14,...49

Required smallest subset is A∪B.

So, nA∪B=nA+nB-nA∩B
=25+7-3
=29.

subject: Mathematics
q: An organization awarded <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn></math> medals in event <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>A</mi><none/><mo>'</mo><mprescripts/><none/><mo>'</mo></mmultiscripts></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> in event <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>B</mi><none/><mo>'</mo><mprescripts/><none/><mo>'</mo></mmultiscripts></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn></math> in event <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><none/><mo>'</mo><mprescripts/><none/><mo>'</mo></mmultiscripts></math>. If these medals went to total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> men and only five men got medals in all the three events, then, how many received medals in exactly two of three events?
question: An organization awarded <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn></math> medals in event <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>A</mi><none/><mo>'</mo><mprescripts/><none/><mo>'</mo></mmultiscripts></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> in event <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>B</mi><none/><mo>'</mo><mprescripts/><none/><mo>'</mo></mmultiscripts></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn></math> in event <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><none/><mo>'</mo><mprescripts/><none/><mo>'</mo></mmultiscripts></math>. If these medals went to total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> men and only five men got medals in all the three events, then, how many received medals in exactly two of three events?
solution:
Let A, B and C denote the set of men who received medals in even A, event B and event C respectively.

Then, nA=48, nB=25, nC=18 , nA∪B∪C=60 and nA∩B∩C=5,

Now we know that, nA∪B∪C=nA+nB+nC-nA∩B-nA∩C-nB∩C+nA∩B∩C

⇒60=48+25+18-n(A∩B)-n(A∩C)-n(B∩C)+5

⇒n(A∩B)+n(A∩C)+n(A∩C)=48+25+18+5-60=36

Therefore, the number of people who received medals in exactly two of the three sports will be 36-3A∩B∩C=36-15=21.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{A}=\left\{x:\left|x^{2}-10\right| \leq 6\right\}$ and $\mathrm{B}=\{x:|x-2|>1\}$. Then
question: Let $\mathrm{A}=\left\{x:\left|x^{2}-10\right| \leq 6\right\}$ and $\mathrm{B}=\{x:|x-2|>1\}$. Then
solution:
$A = \{x : |x^2 - 10| \le 6\}$: $-6 \le x^2 - 10 \le 6 \Rightarrow 4 \le x^2 \le 16$, so $A = [-4, -2] \cup [2, 4]$.
$B = \{x : |x - 2| > 1\} = (-\infty, 1) \cup (3, \infty)$.
$A^c = (-\infty, -4) \cup (-2, 2) \cup (4, \infty)$.
$B - A = B \cap A^c = [(-\infty, 1) \cap A^c] \cup [(3, \infty) \cap A^c]$
$= [(-\infty, -4) \cup (-2, 1)] \cup [(4, \infty)]$
$= (-\infty, -4) \cup (-2, 1) \cup (4, \infty)$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of elements in the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℤ</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is _______ .
question: The number of elements in the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℤ</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is _______ .
solution:
Given,

n2-10n+19<6

Now we know that,

x<a⇒-a<x<a

Now using above formula we get,

⇒-6<n2-10n+19<6

⇒n2-10n+25>0 and n2-10n+13<0

⇒n-52>0 and n-5-23n-5+23<0

⇒n∈ℤ-5 and n∈2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8

Hence, n=2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let A=1,2,3,...20. Let R1 and R2 two relation on A such that

R1={a,b:b is divisible by a}

R2={a,b:a is an integral multiple of b}

Then, number of elements in R1−R2 is equal to __________.
question:
Let A=1,2,3,...20. Let R1 and R2 two relation on A such that

R1={a,b:b is divisible by a}

R2={a,b:a is an integral multiple of b}

Then, number of elements in R1−R2 is equal to __________.
solution:
Given: A=1,2,3,...20, R1={a,b:b is divisible by a} and R2={a,b:a is an integral multiple of b}.

⇒R1=1,1,1,2,...,1,20,2,2,2,4,...,2,20,3,3,3,6,...,3,18,4,4,4,8,...,4,20,5,5,5,10,...,5,20,6,6,6,18,...,6,18,7,7,7,14,8,8,8,16,9,9,9,18,10,10,10,20,11,11,...,20,20

⇒nR1=20+10+6+5+4+3+2+2+2+2+10=66

⇒R1∩R2=1,1,2,2,3,3,4,4,...,20,20

⇒nR1∩R2=20

⇒nR1-R2=nR1-nR1∩R2

⇒nR1-R2=66-20

⇒nR1-R2=46

question: Let ∪i=150Xi=∪i=1nYi=T, where each Xi contains 10 elements and each Yi contains 5 elements. If each element of the set T is an element of exactly 20 of sets Xi's and exactly 6 of sets Yi's then n is equal to :
solution:
∪i=150Xi=∪i=1nYi=T

From question's conditions

10×5020=5n6

⇒n=30

subject: Mathematics
q: In a school, there are three types of games to be played. Some of the students play two types of games, but none play all the three games. Which Venn diagrams can justify the above statement?
question: In a school, there are three types of games to be played. Some of the students play two types of games, but none play all the three games. Which Venn diagrams can justify the above statement?
solution:
In P, the shaded region is lying in all the circles. It means some students play all the games.

In Q, the shaded region is lying in all the circles. It means some students play all the games.

In R, the shaded region is lying in all the circles. It means some students play all the games.

Hence, none of the Venn diagrams justify the statement.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let A be the set of all functions $f: \mathbf{Z} \rightarrow \mathbf{Z}$ and R be a relation on A such that $\mathrm{R}=\{(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{g}): f(0)=\mathrm{g}(1)$ and $f(1)=\mathrm{g}(0)\}$. Then R is:
question: Let A be the set of all functions $f: \mathbf{Z} \rightarrow \mathbf{Z}$ and R be a relation on A such that $\mathrm{R}=\{(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{g}): f(0)=\mathrm{g}(1)$ and $f(1)=\mathrm{g}(0)\}$. Then R is:
options: Symmetric and transitive but not reflective
Symmetric but neither reflective nor transitive
Reflexive but neither symmetric nor transitive
Transitive but neither reflexive nor symmetric
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}=\{(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{g}): \mathrm{f}(0)=\mathrm{g}(1) \text { and } \mathrm{f}(1)=\mathrm{g}(0)\} \\ & \text { Reflexive: }(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{f}) \in \mathrm{R} \\ & =\mathrm{f}(0)=\mathrm{f}(1) \text { and } \mathrm{f}(1)=\mathrm{f}(0) \rightarrow \text { must hold } \\ & \Rightarrow \text { but this is not true for all function }\end{aligned}$
so not reflexive
Symmetric: If $(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{g}) \in \mathrm{R} \Rightarrow(\mathrm{g}, \mathrm{f}) \in \mathrm{R}$
Now, $g(0)=f(1)$ and $g(1)=f(0) \rightarrow$ true
$\therefore$ symmetric
Transitive : $\operatorname{If}(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{g}) \in \mathrm{R}$ and $(\mathrm{g}, \mathrm{h}) \in \mathrm{R}$
$\Rightarrow(\mathrm{f}, \mathrm{h}) \in \mathrm{R}$
Now $(f, g) \in R \Rightarrow f(0)=g(1)$ and $f(1)=g(0)$
$(\mathrm{g}, \mathrm{h}) \in \mathrm{R} \Rightarrow \mathrm{g}(0)=\mathrm{h}(1)$ and $\mathrm{g}(1)=\mathrm{h}(0)$
For $(f, h) \in R$ we need $f(0)=h(1)$ and $f(1)=h(0)$
Now $f(0)=g(1)=h(0)$ and $f(1)=g(0)=h(1)$
Hence not transitive

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3, \ldots, 10\}$ and R be a relation on A such that $\mathrm{R}=\{(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}): \mathrm{a}=2 \mathrm{~b}+1\}$. Let $\left(\mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{a}_2\right)$, $\left(a_2, a_3\right),\left(a_3, a_4\right), \ldots .,\left(a_k, a_{k+1}\right)$ be a sequence of $k$ elements of R such that the second entry of an ordered pair is equal to the first entry of the next ordered pair. Then the largest integer k , for which such a sequence exists, is equal to :
question: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3, \ldots, 10\}$ and R be a relation on A such that $\mathrm{R}=\{(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}): \mathrm{a}=2 \mathrm{~b}+1\}$. Let $\left(\mathrm{a}_1, \mathrm{a}_2\right)$, $\left(a_2, a_3\right),\left(a_3, a_4\right), \ldots .,\left(a_k, a_{k+1}\right)$ be a sequence of $k$ elements of R such that the second entry of an ordered pair is equal to the first entry of the next ordered pair. Then the largest integer k , for which such a sequence exists, is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{a}=2 \mathrm{~b}+1 \\ & 2 \mathrm{~b}=\mathrm{a}-1 \\ & \mathrm{R}=\{(3,1),(5,2), \ldots,(99,49)\}
\end{aligned}$
Let $(2 m+1, m),(2 \lambda-1, \lambda)$ are such ordered pairs.
According to the condition
$\mathrm{m}=2 \lambda-1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~m}=\text { odd number }$
$\Rightarrow 1^{\text {st }}$ element of ordered pair $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b})$
$a=2(2 \lambda-1)+1=4 \lambda-1$
Hence $a \in\{3,7, \ldots, 99\}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda \in\{1,2, \ldots, 25\}$
$\Rightarrow$ set of sequence
$\begin{aligned} & \left\{(4 \lambda-1,2 \lambda-1),(2 \lambda-1, \lambda-1),\left(\lambda-1, \frac{\lambda-2}{2}\right), \ldots \ldots .\right\} \\ & 2^{\text {nd }} \text { element of each ordered pair }=\frac{\lambda-2^{\mathrm{r}-2}}{2^{\mathrm{r}-2}}\end{aligned}$
For maximum number of ordered pairs in such sequence
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\lambda-2^{\mathrm{r}-2}}{2^{\mathrm{r}-2}}=1 \text { or } 2 ; 1 \leq \lambda \leq 25 \\ & \lambda=2^{\mathrm{r}-1} \text { or } \lambda=3.2^{\mathrm{r}-2}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Case-I : } \lambda=2 \mathrm{r}-1 \\ & \lambda=2,2^2, 2^3, 2^4 \\ & r=2,3,4,5\end{aligned}$
Hence maximum value of $r$ is 5 when $\lambda=16$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Case-II }: \lambda=3.2^{\mathrm{r}-2} \\ & \lambda=3,6,12,24 \\ & \mathrm{r}=2, \quad 3, \quad 4,5\end{aligned}$
Hence maximum value of $r$ is 5 when $\lambda=24$

subject: Mathematics
q: The relation $R=\{(x, y): x, y \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $x+y$ is even $\}$ is:
question: The relation $R=\{(x, y): x, y \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $x+y$ is even $\}$ is:
options: reflexive and symmetric but not transitive
symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
solution:
For reflexive $(x, x) \in \mathbb{R}, x \in \mathbb{Z}$
$\Rightarrow x+x=2 x \rightarrow \text { even }$
For symmetric of $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}$ then $(y, x) \in \mathbb{R}$ when $x, y \in \mathbb{Z}$
$x+y \rightarrow$ even
$\Rightarrow y+x \rightarrow \text { even }$
for transitive if $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R} \Rightarrow x+y \rightarrow$ even
$(y, z) \in \mathbb{R} \Rightarrow y+z \rightarrow \text { even }$
$x+2 y+z \rightarrow$ even
$\Rightarrow x+z$ is even
$\Rightarrow(x, z) \in \mathbb{R}$
$\Rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is an equivalence relation.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>Z</mi></math> be the set of integers. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>Z</mi><mtext> </mtext><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>Z</mi><mtext>  </mtext><mo>:</mo><mo>-</mo><mtext> </mtext><mn>3</mn><mo><</mo><mtext> </mtext><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo><</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>}</mo></math>, then the number of subsets of the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>B</mi></math>, is :
question: Let <math><mi>Z</mi></math> be the set of integers. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>Z</mi><mtext> </mtext><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>Z</mi><mtext>  </mtext><mo>:</mo><mo>-</mo><mtext> </mtext><mn>3</mn><mo><</mo><mtext> </mtext><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo><</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>}</mo></math>, then the number of subsets of the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>B</mi></math>, is :
solution:
Given 2x+2x-2x-3=1=20⇒x=-2, 2, 3.

∴nA=3

Again, -3<2x-1<9⇒-1<x<5

⇒x=0, 1, 2, 3, 4

∴nB=5

∴n A×B=3×5=15

∴Number of subset of A×B=215
